item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I opened the song in another tab after it was linked, and it ended exactly as I read the last word. It might have been the music, but I think the final section had some really great atmosphere. Very cool.

item.title: broken links?
item.content:encoded: hey the article links at the bottom are for 4202 and 4204 rather than 4203 and 4205, so it just loops when you try to go to the next one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice touch with the wolf’s-bane, compelling narrative in such a short article. Loved it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First to get this aside: how did they carbon-dated a coin? lead-iron-pyrite covered with gold? There is no carbon to date from.

For critique regarding the article, i mostly agree with the poster above. The ending note is an overdone concept, but the overall idea saves it.

UIU seems competent for once, but a researcher easily stealing an SCP seems quite lolFoundation to me…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The object on its own is pretty boring, kinda in the vein of dnd object catalogue, but it’s really cool how you added a lot more depth to it in the second half. The story is intriguing and thankfully not completely spelled out. It’s especially admirable with such a short article. I admit, I find The song reference seems a bit silly, but I understand why it is there.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Criticism
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this one!

Tanhony rambles
TURN IT OFF

A few things that jumped out at me:

No personnel used in tests of SCP-4204 are permitted to leave Site-11 under any circumstance, effective April 2007.

This is a nice hook in the containment procedures - lets us know that some fucky shit is going on with people who have been involved with 4204, and the fact that these procedures weren’t put in into later on lets us know straight away that whatever happened was an unexpected development.

Procedure-4204:

I really like the specificity of the procedure here to activate 4204. It gives the feeling of a magical ritual being described meticulously as a clinical procedure, and sets a good mystical tone for the object.

The first instance of SCP-4204 was documented in 1998, 13 years prior to the release of this song. Shawn James has no knowledge of the song's inspiration, nor the origins of SCP-4204.

I really like this detail. It doesn’t make any logical sense, of course, and that very fact makes the anomaly feel like a ‘glitch’ in reality in every way.

Such a crime committed for love is still heresy against nature. Who are you to pluck the moon from the sky?

Oh, this is great. The note left here gives the feeling of an entity that will punish you for using the object no matter what, but is empathetic enough to lessen your punishment depending on why you used the object. We don’t get much interaction with the entity here, but that’s for the best. Like a lot of SCP files that I enjoy, it feels like we’re just brushing up against a much bigger story.

+1

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to Tanhony or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending did it for me. It was a sharp turn, both heart-warming (in a twisted way) and heart-breaking. +1

item.title: Re: From the Snack Covered Desk of the Author
item.content:encoded: Thanks to HouseofBalloons and Laneous for fixing the title and the SCP-XXXX issues respectively. You have my eternal adoration

item.title: From the Snack Covered Desk of the Author
item.content:encoded: AH! Here we go! My first attempt at a Bright's Challenge. Wrote this up in about two hours listening to this on repeat. Hope you enjoy it
